list: If you’re overloading assignment (=), subscript ([]), function call (()), or member selection (->), do so as a member function.If you’re overloading a unary operator, do so as a member function.If you’re overloading a binary operator that modifies its left operand (e.g. operator+=), do so as a member function if you can.If you’re overloading a binary operator that does not modify its left operand (e.g. operator+), do so as a normal function or friend function.

If you’re overloading a unary operator, do so as a member function.If you’re overloading a binary operator that modifies its left operand (e.g. operator+=), do so as a member function if you can.If you’re overloading a binary operator that does not modify its left operand (e.g. operator+), do so as a normal function or friend function.

If you’re overloading a binary operator that modifies its left operand (e.g. operator+=), do so as a member function if you can.If you’re overloading a binary operator that does not modify its left operand (e.g. operator+), do so as a normal function or friend function.
If you’re overloading a binary operator that does not modify its left operand (e.g. operator+), do so as a normal function or friend function.

If a new object has to be created before the copying can occur, the copy constructor is used (note: this includes passing or returning objects by value).If a new object does not have to be created before the copying can occur, the assignment operator is used.
If a new object does not have to be created before the copying can occur, the assignment operator is used.
AssumingPointis a class andpointis an instance of that class, should you use a normal/friend or member function overload for the following operators?

content.text: No. Because using memberwise initialization/copy is fine here, using the compiler provided defaults is acceptable.

intmay be 16-bits on some platforms, which would mean ourAverageobject could only have a maximum numerator value of 32,767. Usingstd::int32_tguarantees a 32-bit integral value, which gives us much more range to work with.

There are many different ways to implement a fixed point number. Because a fixed point number is essentially a subcase of a floating point number (where the number of digits after the decimal is fixed instead of variable), using a floating point number might seem like an obvious choice. But floating point numbers have precision issues. With a fixed number of decimal digits, we can reasonably enumerate all the possible fractional values (in our case, .00 to .99), so using a data type that has precision issues isn’t the best choice.A better solution would be to use a 16-bit signed integer to hold the non-fractional part of the number, and an 8-bit signed integer to hold the fractional component.
A better solution would be to use a 16-bit signed integer to hold the non-fractional part of the number, and an 8-bit signed integer to hold the fractional component.

list: Clamp the fraction portion (if >99, set to 99).Treat overflow as relevant (if >99, reduce by 100 and add 1 to base).
Treat overflow as relevant (if >99, reduce by 100 and add 1 to base).
